description: 88% of prior auth requests are partially or entirely manual. Physicians spend 1.1hrs/week, nurses 13.1hrs/week, clerical staff 5.6hrs/week on PA. Costs $23-31B annually across US healthcare system.
who_suffers: Physicians, nurses, medical office staff, patients
current_process: Staff complete PA forms (often fax-based), submit to insurers, wait days-weeks for response, handle denials and appeals manually. Up to 800 services may require PA per insurer.
why_manual: Fragmented payer requirements; each insurer has different forms and criteria; lack of standardized electronic submission (CMS rule mandating APIs not effective until 2027)

description: When PA requests are denied, providers must gather additional clinical documentation, draft appeal letters, and resubmit -- often multiple times. Denial reasons are frequently vague, requiring back-and-forth with payers.
who_suffers: Physicians, practice managers, patients waiting for care
current_process: Manual review of denial reason, gathering supporting clinical evidence, writing appeal letters, faxing/calling payers, tracking appeal status
why_manual: Each case requires clinical judgment to match documentation to payer criteria; denial reasons vary by payer; no standardized appeal format

description: Each insurer has different PA requirements for different procedures/medications. Practices must maintain knowledge of requirements across dozens of payers and hundreds of procedures, which change frequently.
who_suffers: Practice managers, billing staff, physicians
current_process: Manual lookup of payer-specific requirements via phone calls, payer portals, or fax; maintaining internal reference documents that quickly become outdated
why_manual: Requirements change frequently; no universal database; each payer maintains its own criteria in different formats

description: ICD-10 has ~70,000 diagnosis codes and CPT has ~10,000 procedure codes. Coders must select exactly the right codes from clinical documentation to ensure proper reimbursement and avoid audit penalties.
who_suffers: Medical coders, billing departments, healthcare providers
current_process: Trained coders read clinical notes, interpret diagnoses and procedures, manually assign ICD-10 and CPT codes, cross-reference payer rules for bundling/unbundling
why_manual: Requires understanding of complex medical terminology and payer-specific rules; codes update annually; subjective interpretation of clinical documentation

description: Claim denial rates are rising across the industry. With 441M+ remits analyzed across 1500 hospitals, denials put billions in revenue at risk. Each denied claim requires manual review, root cause analysis, correction, and resubmission.
who_suffers: Revenue cycle teams, billing departments, hospital CFOs
current_process: Staff review denied claims, identify denial reason codes, correct errors or gather missing info, resubmit claims, track status, escalate to appeals if needed
why_manual: Denial reasons are complex and varied (eligibility, coding errors, missing info, medical necessity); each requires different corrective action

description: The billing process involves patient registration, eligibility verification, code assignment, claim creation, submission, and payment posting -- a multi-step workflow spanning three parties with frequent errors.
who_suffers: Medical billers, practice administrators, patients
current_process: Manual data entry across multiple systems; verification of patient insurance; matching codes to documentation; submitting claims electronically or on paper; reconciling payments
why_manual: Multiple disconnected systems (EHR, practice management, clearinghouses); payer-specific formatting requirements; frequent data entry errors

description: Financial institutions must verify identity (CIP), perform customer due diligence (CDD), and enhanced due diligence (EDD) for high-risk clients. Process involves collecting documents, verifying against databases, assessing risk profiles.
who_suffers: Compliance officers, bank operations staff, fintech onboarding teams
current_process: Manual collection of ID documents, proof of address; verification against government databases; beneficial owner identification for entities; risk profiling; ongoing monitoring
why_manual: Regulatory complexity across jurisdictions; each customer type requires different verification depth; beneficial ownership structures can be complex

pain_name: AML Transaction Monitoring and SAR Filing
description: Banks must continuously monitor transactions for suspicious patterns and file Suspicious Activity Reports (SARs). High false-positive rates (often 90%+) mean analysts spend most time clearing legitimate transactions.
who_suffers: AML compliance analysts, compliance officers, bank risk teams
current_process: Automated alerts generated by rule-based systems produce massive false positives; analysts manually investigate each alert, gather context, document findings, file SARs when warranted
why_manual: Rule-based systems lack contextual understanding; investigation requires pulling data from multiple systems; narrative writing for SARs is time-intensive

description: Over 1 billion B2B invoices processed weekly with 97% still manual as of recent estimates. Average cost $5-$15 per invoice. 10% paid too late for early payment discounts; ~2% contain errors.
who_suffers: AP clerks, finance teams, controllers
current_process: Receive invoice, match to purchase order and packing slip (three-way match), get approval, enter into accounting system, schedule payment, reconcile
why_manual: Invoices arrive in varied formats (paper, PDF, email); matching requires cross-referencing multiple documents; approval chains are organizational

pain_name: Invoice Fraud Detection in Accounts Payable
description: AP departments must watch for fraudulent invoices, duplicate payments, and vendor impersonation. Without purchase order systems, the first line of defense is manual manager review.
who_suffers: AP staff, controllers, CFOs
current_process: Manual review of invoices against known vendors, checking for duplicates, verifying legitimacy of new vendor requests, separating duties between vendor creation and payment
why_manual: Fraudsters use sophisticated tactics (look-alike invoices, vendor impersonation); detection requires cross-referencing multiple data sources

description: CDI specialists bridge providers and coders to ensure documentation accurately reflects patient acuity and supports correct DRG assignment. 90% of hospitals with strong CDI programs earned $1.5M+ more in reimbursements. Requires reviewing every inpatient chart.
who_suffers: CDI specialists, physicians, inpatient coders
current_process: CDI specialists review charts, query physicians for clarification on diagnoses, ensure documentation supports coding accuracy, track query response rates
why_manual: Physicians focus on clinical care not documentation specifics; coding requires precise language that clinicians don't naturally use; each chart needs individual review

pain_name: Contract Review and Risk Identification for Small Law Firms
description: Small law firms handle contract review manually -- reading every clause, identifying risks, comparing to standard terms, and flagging deviations. A single M&A due diligence can involve reviewing hundreds of contracts. 42% of enterprises cite risk assessment as top CLM driver.
who_suffers: Associates at small/mid-size law firms, in-house legal teams, paralegals
current_process: Junior lawyers manually read contracts, create summaries, flag non-standard clauses, compare to templates, compile findings into reports
why_manual: Each contract is unique; risk identification requires legal judgment; volume overwhelms small teams; no standardized risk taxonomy across firms

description: Due diligence for M&A involves reviewing 9+ audit areas across financial, legal, labor, tax, IT, and environmental domains. Reviewing a data room of thousands of documents is extremely time-intensive and error-prone.
who_suffers: M&A attorneys, corporate development teams, PE fund associates
current_process: Teams of lawyers manually review data room documents, extract key terms, identify risks, compile findings into DD reports; often under tight timelines
why_manual: Volume of documents is massive; each requires domain expertise; cross-referencing across audit areas is complex; tight deal timelines increase pressure

description: New hire onboarding involves collecting and processing tax forms (W-4, I-9), benefits enrollment, policy acknowledgments, equipment provisioning, IT access setup, and training assignments. With 25% of US workers being newcomers at any time, this is a constant workflow.
who_suffers: HR generalists, hiring managers, new employees
current_process: HR manually sends forms, collects signatures, verifies I-9 documents, enrolls in benefits systems, creates accounts across multiple IT systems, tracks completion
why_manual: Multiple disconnected systems (HRIS, benefits, payroll, IT); regulatory requirements (I-9 within 3 days); each new hire generates 10-20 discrete tasks across departments

description: After contract execution, organizations must track obligations, deadlines, renewal dates, and compliance requirements across potentially thousands of active contracts. Missing a deadline can trigger penalties or auto-renewals.
who_suffers: Contract managers, legal ops, procurement teams
current_process: Manual tracking via spreadsheets or basic CLM tools; periodic reviews of contract terms; calendar reminders for key dates; ad-hoc compliance checks
why_manual: Contract portfolios grow faster than staff; terms are buried in unstructured documents; no automated extraction of obligations from contract text

description: Each import shipment requires correct tariff classification, agency compliance documentation (FDA, USDA, Fish & Wildlife), and customs declarations. Misclassification leads to delays, seizures, fines. Brokers must know thousands of tariff codes and per-agency requirements.
who_suffers: Customs brokers, importers, freight forwarders
current_process: Licensed customs brokers manually classify goods using Harmonized Tariff Schedule, prepare entry documents, determine applicable duties, file declarations with CBP, coordinate with multiple government agencies
why_manual: Tariff schedule has 10,000+ line items; each agency has different requirements; rules change frequently; classification requires product-specific knowledge

description: Importers face risk of fines and penalties for incorrect declarations. Customs brokers must maintain detailed records for audit purposes. Each shipment may involve multiple agencies with overlapping requirements.
who_suffers: Importers, customs brokers, compliance officers
current_process: Manual tracking of regulatory changes; maintaining documentation per CBP requirements; responding to audits with historical records; monitoring for sanctions compliance
why_manual: Regulations change frequently across multiple agencies; FCPA and sanctions add complexity; record-keeping requirements span years

description: Title searches require retrieving physical or scanned documents from county records, tracing chain of title through grantor-grantee indexes, identifying all liens/easements/encumbrances, and compiling an abstract. Process varies by county and many records are not digitized.
who_suffers: Title abstractors, title companies, real estate attorneys
current_process: Trained abstractors manually search county records (often in-person), retrieve and analyze each recorded document, identify parties and obligations, compile findings into title abstract reports
why_manual: Records in varied formats (physical books, scans, some digital); no standardized cross-county database; each document requires legal interpretation; chain-of-title analysis is sequential

description: When title searches reveal defects (unresolved liens, breaks in chain of title, boundary disputes), clearing them requires legal research, contacting prior parties, filing corrective documents, and sometimes litigation.
who_suffers: Title companies, real estate attorneys, property buyers
current_process: Manual legal research to identify defect source; contacting previous owners or lienholders; preparing and filing corrective instruments (quitclaim deeds, lien releases); court proceedings if needed
why_manual: Each defect is unique; resolution requires legal judgment and often involves third parties; some defects require court action

pain_name: Insurance Underwriting Data Gathering and Risk Assessment
description: Underwriters must gather financial statements, credit reports, loss history, property data, and other risk factors from multiple sources to assess risk and price policies. Commercial underwriting is especially data-intensive.
who_suffers: Insurance underwriters, actuaries, account managers
current_process: Underwriters manually collect data from applicants, third-party databases, loss runs, financial statements; input into rating models; apply underwriting guidelines; document decisions
why_manual: Data comes from many disconnected sources; each line of business has different risk factors; judgment calls required for non-standard risks

description: Building permit applications require preparing detailed plans, demonstrating code compliance, submitting to local authorities, responding to plan check comments, and managing the approval timeline. Process varies by jurisdiction.
who_suffers: General contractors, architects, property developers
current_process: Manual preparation of permit applications; submitting architectural plans; responding to plan reviewer comments; tracking application status across multiple departments (building, fire, zoning, environmental)
why_manual: Each jurisdiction has different requirements and processes; plan reviewers may have subjective interpretations of code; multi-department coordination is fragmented

description: Educational institutions must compile extensive self-study reports for accrediting bodies, gathering data on outcomes, curricula, faculty, finances, and student achievement across multiple departments. Reports can be hundreds of pages.
who_suffers: University administrators, department chairs, accreditation coordinators, institutional research staff
current_process: Staff manually compile data from across departments; write narrative self-assessments; gather evidence portfolios; format per accreditor requirements; coordinate reviews across stakeholders
why_manual: Data lives in many disconnected systems (SIS, LMS, HR, finance); each accreditor has different standards and formats; reporting cycles are multi-year but preparation is intensive

description: Nonprofits and researchers must write detailed grant proposals with 7+ sections (abstract, narrative, methodology, budget, evaluation). Each funder has different requirements. Post-award reporting adds ongoing burden. Growing use of third-party grant writers signals the pain.
who_suffers: Nonprofit program directors, researchers, grant writers, development staff
current_process: Staff research funders, analyze RFPs, write proposals (often 20-50 pages), compile budgets, gather supporting documents, submit through funder portals, then report on outcomes post-award
why_manual: Each funder has unique requirements and formats; proposals require deep domain knowledge + persuasive writing; post-award reporting requires tracking outcomes against promised deliverables

description: Federal agencies must process FOIA requests by searching for responsive records, reviewing for exemptions, redacting sensitive information, and responding within statutory timeframes. Backlogs are common and politically contentious.
who_suffers: FOIA officers, agency records managers, general counsel staff
current_process: Staff receive requests, search across agency records systems, retrieve documents, review each page for 9 exemption categories, redact exempt information, prepare response letters, track status
why_manual: Records scattered across systems and physical files; each document requires individual review for exemptions; legal judgment needed for redaction decisions; volume exceeds staff capacity

pain_name: USDA Subsidy and Conservation Program Applications
description: Farmers must navigate complex USDA programs (FSA loans, NRCS conservation programs, crop insurance) with extensive paperwork, eligibility requirements, and compliance documentation. Small farmers often lack resources to complete applications.
who_suffers: Small/medium farmers, farm service agency staff, agricultural consultants
current_process: Farmers visit FSA offices, complete multi-page applications, provide financial documentation, demonstrate eligibility, comply with conservation requirements, file annual reports
why_manual: Programs have complex eligibility rules; paperwork burden disproportionately affects small farms; each program has different forms and requirements; FSA offices are understaffed
